summaries.1: Chapter I sets the AI Act’s subject matter, scope, definitions, and relationship with other EU law — including when the rules apply and key concepts such as AI system, provider, and deployer.
summaries.2: Chapter II lists prohibited AI practices that are unacceptable in the Union, including certain manipulative, exploitative, and social-scoring uses of AI.
summaries.3: Chapter III is the core high-risk framework: classification, requirements, conformity assessment, registration, and obligations for providers and deployers of high-risk AI systems.
summaries.4: Chapter IV imposes transparency obligations for certain AI systems (e.g. chatbots, deepfakes, emotion recognition) so users know they interact with AI.
summaries.5: Chapter V regulates general-purpose AI models, including transparency, copyright, and systemic-risk obligations for the most capable models.
summaries.6: Chapter VI supports innovation through regulatory sandboxes, real-world testing, and measures to reduce burdens on SMEs and start-ups.
summaries.7: Chapter VII establishes governance: the AI Office, Board, scientific panel, and national competent authorities coordinating application of the Act.
summaries.8: Chapter VIII requires an EU database for high-risk AI systems registered under the Act.
summaries.9: Chapter IX covers post-market monitoring, incident reporting, market surveillance, and enforcement cooperation among authorities.
summaries.10: Chapter X encourages codes of conduct and guidelines to foster trustworthy AI beyond minimum legal requirements.
summaries.11: Chapter XI governs delegated acts and committee procedures for updating technical elements of the framework.
summaries.12: Chapter XII sets administrative fines and penalties for infringements of the AI Act.
summaries.13: Chapter XIII contains final provisions, including evaluation, review, and staged dates of application for different chapters.
